questions.question: Inside a child component, what is the correct way to treat the `props` object?
options: As read-only — never assign to `props.x`; mutating it is a bug
As mutable local state you can freely reassign to update the UI
As a copy that, when mutated, automatically re-renders the parent
As a two-way binding that pushes changes back to the parent on assignment
questions.explanation: Props are read-only inputs; the child renders from them but must never mutate them. Mutating props breaks React's rendering assumptions and is a bug — assignment does not trigger re-renders or propagate to the parent.

questions.question: A child needs to change a value that the parent owns. What is the idiomatic React mechanism?
options: The child invokes a function prop (callback) passed by the parent, which updates the parent's state
The child directly reassigns the parent's variable through a ref
The child mutates the prop in place so the parent observes the change
The child calls `setState` on the parent component instance
questions.explanation: The pattern is "data down, events up": the child calls a function prop (e.g. `onChange`); the parent updates *its* state and the new value flows back down. A child cannot reach up and change a parent's variable directly.

questions.question: In React's data model, when a parent re-renders due to a state change, it produces fresh props for its children and React re-renders the affected subtree with the new values.
questions.explanation: Re-render propagation works exactly this way: a parent's state change produces fresh props, and React reconciles and re-renders the affected child subtree with the new values.

questions.question: Given `function C({ n = 0 })`, for which passed value of the `n` prop does the default `0` take effect?
options: Only when `n` is `undefined`
When `n` is `undefined` or `null`
When `n` is `undefined`, `null`, `0`, or `""`
Whenever `n` is any falsy value
questions.explanation: A destructuring default applies only when the value is `undefined`. It does NOT apply for `null`, `0`, or `""` — those are passed through as-is even though some are falsy.

questions.question: As of React 19, `defaultProps` on function components has been removed, so you should use destructuring parameter defaults instead.
questions.explanation: React 19 removed `defaultProps` for function components; parameter (destructuring) defaults are the supported replacement. (It still applies to class components, which the reviewer does not cover here.)

questions.question: In the JSX `<button {...rest} onClick={handleClick} />`, where `rest` also contains an `onClick`, which `onClick` is used?
options: `handleClick`, because the explicit attribute appears after the spread and overrides it
The `onClick` from `rest`, because spread entries always win over explicit attributes
Neither — React throws on the duplicate `onClick`
Both fire in source order, since React merges duplicate handlers
questions.explanation: Order matters: later JSX attributes override earlier spread entries. Since `onClick={handleClick}` appears after `{...rest}`, it wins. React does not throw on or merge duplicates — last write wins.

questions.question: Why is blindly spreading `{...props}` onto a native DOM element (e.g. `<div {...props} />`) risky?
options: Component-specific props can leak onto the DOM node, and React warns about unknown DOM attributes
Spreading onto a DOM element silently mutates the original props object
React refuses to render any element that receives a spread of more than one prop
The DOM element will re-render its parent every time any spread prop changes
questions.explanation: Spreading everything can leak your component-specific (non-HTML) props onto the DOM node, and React warns about unknown DOM attributes. The fix is to pull off your own props first (`const { title, ...rest } = props`) and spread only `rest`.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid React `children` values that render without throwing? (Select all that apply.)
options: A string or number
`null`, `false`, or `undefined` (rendered as nothing)
A function (the render-prop pattern)
A plain object like `{ a: 1 }`
questions.explanation: `children` may be a string/number, an element, an array/fragment, `null`/`false`/`undefined` (rendered as nothing), or a function (render prop). A plain object is NOT a valid React child and throws.

questions.question: Whatever markup you nest between a component's opening and closing tags is delivered to that component as the special `children` prop.
questions.explanation: Nested content arrives as `props.children`. This is the mechanism that lets layout/wrapper components render content they don't know ahead of time.

questions.question: Given the `Card` component, what does `rest` contain and where does it go?
options: `rest` holds `className="featured"` (and other HTML attrs), which is spread onto the `div`; the spread's `className` overrides the literal `"card"`
`rest` holds `title` and `children`, which are forwarded to the `div`
`rest` is empty because `title` and `children` consume every prop
`rest` holds `className="featured"`, but the literal `className="card"` overrides it because it is written first
questions.explanation: `title` and `children` are destructured out, so `rest` carries the remaining HTML attributes like `className="featured"`. Because `{...rest}` appears after `className="card"`, the spread's `className` overrides the literal one, so the rendered class is `"featured"`.

questions.question: The reviewer warns against copying a prop into state. When is doing so actually acceptable?
options: When you deliberately want a one-time snapshot, while remaining aware of staleness
Whenever you want the child to render faster than reading the prop directly
Always — copying props into state is the recommended default for performance
Never under any circumstances; it is forbidden by React
questions.explanation: Keep each piece of state in its lowest common owner and pass it down. Copying a prop into state is acceptable only when you deliberately want a snapshot — and even then you must beware of staleness when the prop later changes.

questions.question: Which TypeScript type is the correct, widest choice for a `children` prop that may be elements, strings, numbers, arrays, or `null`?
options: `React.ReactNode`
`React.ReactElement`
`JSX.Element`
`string | number`
questions.explanation: `React.ReactNode` is the widest "anything renderable" type. `React.ReactElement` (or `JSX.Element`) is narrower — use it only when you specifically require a single element.

questions.question: What is `PropsWithChildren<{ title: string }>` equivalent to?
options: `{ title: string; children?: React.ReactNode }`
`{ title: string; children: React.ReactNode }` (children required)
`{ title?: string; children?: React.ReactNode }`
`{ title: string }` plus a required `key` prop
questions.explanation: `PropsWithChildren<T>` adds an OPTIONAL `children: ReactNode` to `T`, so the result is `{ title: string; children?: ReactNode }`. The `children` field is optional, not required.

questions.question: What happens to runtime `propTypes` declarations in React 19, and what is the recommended replacement?
options: They are removed from React core and silently ignored at runtime; use TypeScript, plus a schema library like Zod for runtime validation of external data
They now throw at runtime on the first mismatch; replace them with `defaultProps`
They are upgraded to compile-time checks; no replacement is needed
They still run but only in production builds; replace them with `React.ReactNode`
questions.explanation: `propTypes` were removed from React core in React 19 and are silently ignored at runtime. Use TypeScript for compile-time checking and a schema library (Zod/Valibot) at the boundary for runtime validation of untrusted external data.

questions.question: Comparing `type` and `interface` for props, which statements are accurate? (Select all that apply.)
options: `type` composes nicely with unions and intersections (`A & B`)
`interface` supports declaration merging
Only `interface` can describe a props object; `type` cannot
You should match whatever the surrounding codebase already uses
questions.explanation: Both work for props. `type` composes well with unions/intersections; `interface` supports declaration merging; and you should match the surrounding codebase. The claim that only `interface` can describe props is false — `type Props = { ... }` works fine.

questions.question: A child stores `count` in `useState(props.count)` instead of reading `props.count` directly. The parent later updates and passes a new `props.count`. What does the child render by default?
options: The stale initial value, because the `useState` initializer only runs on the first render and ignores later prop changes
The new prop value, because `useState` re-syncs to its initializer argument on every render
It throws, because seeding state from a prop is disallowed
Alternating values, because the parent re-render forces the child's state to reset
questions.explanation: This is the staleness trap the reviewer warns about. `useState`'s initializer argument is used only on the first render; subsequent changes to `props.count` do not update the copied state, so the child shows the stale snapshot unless you read the prop directly or explicitly sync.

questions.question: You expose a render-prop API where the parent passes a function as `children` (e.g. `<Mouse>{(pos) => <X y={pos}/>}</Mouse>`). How should you type that `children` prop?
options: `children: (arg: T) => React.ReactNode`
`children: React.ReactNode`
`children: React.ReactElement`
`children: T => void`
questions.explanation: For the render-prop pattern, `children` is a function that returns renderable output, so its type is `(arg: T) => React.ReactNode`. `React.ReactNode`/`ReactElement` describe rendered content, not a function, and a `=> void` callback returns nothing to render.

questions.question:
What is the result of rendering `
{ { name: "Ada" } }
` (a plain object placed directly as a child)?
options: React throws an error, because a plain object is not a valid React child
React renders the string `[object Object]`
React renders `Ada` by reading the object's first string field
React renders nothing and logs a silent warning
questions.explanation: Plain objects are not valid React children and throw ("Objects are not valid as a React child"). Valid children include strings, numbers, elements, arrays/fragments, `null`/`false`/`undefined`, and functions — but not a bare object.
